content.text: JavaScript objects are collections of key–value pairs where values can be any type — including functions. They are the fundamental data structure of the language and the foundation for classes, prototypes, and Angular's component model.

attrs.alt: JavaScript objects — creation patterns, prototype chain, property descriptors, copying
attrs.title: JavaScript objects — creation patterns, prototype chain, property descriptors, copying

content.text: JavaScript offers three primary ways to create objects. All three ultimately produce the same type of object; the differences are in syntax, control over the prototype, and how reuse is expressed.
The most common form. Curly braces create an object and immediately populate its properties.
as its explicit prototype. Used when you need fine control over the prototype chain.
Classes (ES2015+) are the standard way to create objects that share methods via a prototype. TypeScript makes this the default pattern for Angular.
Property access uses dot notation for valid identifiers and bracket notation for dynamic keys or keys with special characters.
Destructuring extracts properties into variables. It can rename, provide defaults, and nest.
JavaScript never implicitly deep-copies objects. Assignments copy references.
only re-renders when an input reference changes. Spread-based updates create new references, triggering detection correctly.
Every object property has a descriptor that controls its behavior. Normally this is transparent, but it matters for configuration objects and immutability.
An Angular service is a plain class. When injected, it is a shared object instance — its properties are shared state across all consumers in the same injector.

<!-- keyvalue pipe — iterate object in template -->
@Component({
  template: \`
    <dl>
      @for (entry of config | keyvalue; track entry.key) {
        <dt>{{ entry.key }}</dt>
        <dd>{{ entry.value }}</dd>
      }
    </dl>
  \`,
})
export class ConfigDisplayComponent {
  config = { timeout: 5000, retries: 3, debug: false };
}
Mutating an object that is shared — use spread { ...obj, key: newVal } for immutable updates
Confusing shallow and deep copy — nested objects share references after { ...obj }
Using for...in without hasOwn check — accidentally iterates inherited properties
Accessing a property on null/undefined — use optional chaining obj?.prop
Expecting JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) for deep copy — it drops functions, undefined, Date objects, and circular refs
namespace provides many utility functions for working with objects. These are the ones you will encounter most in JavaScript and TypeScript.
Every object in JavaScript has an internal
). When you access a property, the runtime walks this chain until it finds the key or reaches
of an object; they have no runtime existence. The prototype chain is a runtime JavaScript concept that interfaces do not affect.
Symbol keys are unique and non-enumerable by default. They are invisible to
— useful for metadata or private-like properties on shared objects.
Getter/setter pairs let you compute properties on demand or validate writes, while keeping the same dot-access syntax for callers.
Angular uses getters internally for signals and computed values:
— the parentheses — actually call a getter that reads the signal's internal value.
JavaScript objects are reference types created with literals,
, or classes. Properties are accessed via dot or bracket notation and can be iterated with
. Destructuring provides concise extraction. Spreading creates shallow copies — use
for deep copies. In Angular, objects as
should be replaced (not mutated) to trigger
